questions.questionText: Mycobacteria are difficult to stain with standard Gram stain due to the high concentration of which substance in their cell walls?
questions.explanation: The waxy mycolic acid requires acid-fast staining (Ziehl-Neelsen).

questions.questionText: The primary lung lesion of initial Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection, usually located in the lower lobes and associated with a hilar lymph node, is the:
questions.explanation: The Ghon complex is a calcified parenchymal lesion and calcified hilar node.

questions.questionText: Which component of M. tuberculosis stimulates the delayed-type hypersensitivity (Type IV) response measured in the PPD test?
questions.explanation: T cells sensitized to these proteins respond by causing local induration.

questions.questionText: Multidrug-resistant Tuberculosis (MDR-TB) is defined as resistance to at least:
questions.explanation: INH and RIF are the two most potent first-line anti-TB drugs.

questions.questionText: The virulence of M. tuberculosis is strongly correlated with its ability to grow in 'serpentine cords'. This is mediated by:
questions.explanation: Cord factor inhibits macrophage maturation and induces TNF-alpha release.

questions.questionText: Leprosy (Hansen's disease) is caused by Mycobacterium leprae. Which form represents a weak cell-mediated (Th1) immune response and high bacterial burden?
questions.explanation: Lepromatous leprosy (Th2 dominant) involves widespread, lion-like facial deformities and high infectivity.

questions.optionA: Cooler regions of the body (skin and peripheral nerves)
questions.explanation: The bacteria thrive at roughly 30°C, leading to peripheral nerve damage and anesthesia.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is considered 'Atypical Mycobacteria' or Non-Tuberculous Mycobacteria (NTM) that frequently causes disseminated disease in late-stage AIDS patients?
questions.explanation: MAC causes severe, widespread infection when CD4 counts drop below 50.

questions.questionText: Which vaccine, used to prevent severe forms of childhood TB, involves a live-attenuated strain of Mycobacterium bovis?
questions.explanation: BCG gives variable protection but prevents TB meningitis in kids.

questions.questionText: A positive IGRA (Interferon-Gamma Release Assay) indicates:
questions.optionA: Active infectious state requiring immediate isolation
questions.optionB: Prior exposure to M. tuberculosis (latent or active infection)
questions.explanation: IGRA measures T-cell release of IFN-y but cannot distinguish latent from active TB. It is not confounded by BCG.

questions.questionText: In Mycobacteria, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Microbiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Microbiology references for Mycobacteria. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: Mycobacteria.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Mycobacteria scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: Mycobacteria.
